narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your upper-middle family income background and full-time commitment to studies. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a threshold that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which ranked quite low compared to the typical student's stronger mid-range performance in that area. This stood out as the most influential element, significantly outweighing others in the model's eyes. Your LSAT score, slightly below the average of past successful students, also nudged the prediction toward failure, though less strongly. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA, a bit lower than the dataset average, added a smaller push in the same direction. Positively, your family income level worked in your favor, helping pull the odds slightly toward passing, as did your solid Year 1 decile, which was right around average and provided a minor boost. Being full-time, like most students, had almost no impact either way. Overall, your profile compared to typical admitted students by having a key dip in later-year grades that overshadowed your other solid traits, making the model cautious. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or strengthening your later academics through targeted courses, then reapply with an updated profile to shift those predictions. We're rooting for you to turn this around and succeed in law. Keep pushing.
